senses.definition: used for saying that a person, thing, situation etc. is not actually like what their description, label, or title suggests

examples.x: The organization was democratic in name only, as the actual decision-making power rested with a single individual.
The honorary title of "ambassador" was given to him, but he was an ambassador in name alone, lacking any diplomatic duties or responsibilities.
The company provided employee benefits on paper, but they were benefits in name only, with numerous restrictions and limitations.
The political party claimed to support equality, but it was egalitarian in name alone, as its policies favored the privileged few.
The committee had a diverse membership in name only, with token representation but little meaningful inclusion or participation.

senses.definition: to act and behave as if everything is fine so that others do not find out about one's problems

examples.x: Despite their financial struggles, the family tried to keep up appearances by hosting lavish parties and buying expensive items.
She smiled and acted cheerful at work to keep up appearances, even though she was going through a difficult time personally.
The politician maintained a polished public image to keep up appearances, carefully managing their public relations.
The crumbling mansion was meticulously maintained on the outside to keep up appearances, concealing the decay within.
The couple's troubled marriage was hidden from friends and family as they tried to keep up appearances of a perfect relationship.

senses.definition: an insincere offer or promise of support, assistance, etc.

examples.x: The politician gave lip service to environmental protection but took no action to address the issue.
The company's commitment to diversity was nothing more than lip service, as their hiring practices remained unchanged.
Despite claiming to support equality, his actions showed that his words were mere lip service.
The manager paid lip service to employee feedback but never implemented any of the suggested changes.
The organization's mission statement included lofty ideals, but it was mostly lip service as their practices contradicted their stated values.

word_list.pronunciation: pˌʊt wˈʌnz mˈʌni wˌɛɹ wˈʌnz mˈaʊθ ɪz

senses.definition: to take action that proves one's sincerity or commitment to what one has said by investing or spending money on it
an evil person who seems nice and friendly

examples.x: The charming new neighbor turned out to be a wolf in sheep's clothing, manipulating everyone for personal gain.
The company's advertisement promising great deals proved to be a wolf in sheep's clothing, as hidden fees and poor quality products were revealed.
The seemingly friendly colleague was a wolf in sheep's clothing, stealing credit for others' work behind their backs.
The online scam presented itself as a legitimate opportunity, but it was a wolf in lamb's clothing, preying on unsuspecting victims.
The politician's promises of change were a wolf in sheep's clothing, as their actions revealed a continuation of corrupt practices.

senses.definition: to fake death by lying on the ground without moving

examples.x: When confronted by the aggressive dog, the cat decided to play dead, hoping the dog would lose interest and leave.
During the argument, he chose to play dead, refusing to engage in the heated exchange.
The child was scared of the monster under the bed and decided to play dead, hoping it would go away.
In order to avoid being caught, the thief played dead by lying motionless on the ground until the police passed by.
When the teacher asked for volunteers, none of the students wanted to participate, so they all played dead by avoiding eye contact.

word_list.pronunciation: pˌʊt ˌɑːn ɐ bɹˈeɪv fˈeɪs fɹˈʌnt

senses.definition: to behave in a way that hides one's unhappiness, worries, or problems
to act calmly in order to hide one's anger, enthusiasm, fear, etc. from others

examples.x: Despite winning the lottery, he decided to play it cool and kept the news to himself.
When asked about the upcoming competition, she played it cool, casually mentioning that she was looking forward to the challenge.
The negotiator played it cool during the intense discussion, maintaining a poker face to hide their true intentions.
Even though he was excited about the job offer, he played it cool during the interview, not revealing his enthusiasm too soon.
She had a crush on him, but she played it cool and acted nonchalant whenever they were together.

senses.definition: to have a bad reputation of calling for help when one does not really need any, and so making oneself untrustworthy

examples.x: After constantly exaggerating about her health issues, her family began to think she was crying wolf and stopped taking her complaints seriously.
The politician's history of making unfounded claims caused many people to view his latest statements with skepticism, accusing him of crying wolf.
The student had a habit of frequently using the excuse of a lost homework assignment, thus crying wolf, so when it actually happened, the teacher was doubtful.
The tabloid's reputation for sensationalizing stories led many readers to dismiss their latest headline as just another instance of crying wolf.
The company's repeated warnings about potential layoffs without following through caused employees to become desensitized, viewing each announcement as crying wolf.

senses.definition: fake display of feelings of sadness, remorse, or sympathy

examples.x: The politician shed crocodile tears during his speech, pretending to empathize with the citizens' struggles while actively working against their interests.
She cried crocodile tears when confronted about her actions, attempting to manipulate others into feeling sorry for her.
The actor's emotional outburst during the interview seemed like crocodile tears, designed to gain sympathy and generate publicity.
Despite his apologies, his actions revealed that his remorse was nothing more than crocodile tears.
The manager shed crocodile tears after firing the employee, pretending to feel sorry while having planned the termination for months.

word_list.word: butter would not melt in {one's} mouth
word_list.pronunciation: bˈʌɾɚ wʊd nˌɑːt mˈɛlt ɪn wˈʌnz mˈaʊθ

senses.definition: used for saying that a person only appears to be nice or innocent, but they are not like that in reality

examples.x: She acted as if butter would not melt in her mouth, but behind closed doors, she was always causing trouble.
Despite his sweet smile, he was a troublemaker at heart, with a mischievous nature that belied the appearance that butter would not melt in his mouth.
The child looked so innocent, as if butter would not melt in her mouth, but she was secretly the one who broke the vase.
The politician spoke with such sincerity, as if butter would not melt in his mouth, but his actions revealed a pattern of corruption and deceit.
She presented herself as the perfect student, as if butter would not melt in her mouth, but she was actually the mastermind behind the cheating scandal.

senses.definition: to talk or behave in a way that is fake
used to refer to when someone does something intentionally, but pretends as if it was an accident

examples.x: Sarah dropped her phone accidentally on purpose to get her crush's attention.
The politician spilled coffee on his opponent during the debate accidentally on purpose to discredit them.
John bumped into his ex-girlfriend at the party accidentally on purpose to gauge her reaction.
The employee accidentally on purpose left confidential documents on the boss's desk to draw attention to a certain
Mary strategically left her notebook behind accidentally on purpose to make her coworker finds it and sees her contact information.

senses.definition: a person sent into a group as a spy to report on its activities

examples.x: The undercover detective used a known criminal as a "stool pigeon" to gather evidence against the gang.
The disgruntled employee acted as a "stool pigeon" and reported his coworkers' unauthorized activities to the management.
The con artist posed as a fellow scam victim to gain the trust of others and later turned into a "stool pigeon" for the police.
The mob boss suspected there was a "stool pigeon" within his organization, leaking information to rival gangs.
The journalist relied on a "stool pigeon" to expose the corrupt practices of the politician.

senses.definition: to pretend as if one is asleep or dead so that others will not bother or attack one

examples.x: When confronted by the bully, Tim decided to play possum and remained silent until the situation defused.
The cunning fox approached the henhouse, but the chickens played possum to avoid drawing attention.
The suspect, realizing the police were closing in, chose to play possum and acted as if he had no knowledge of the crime.
In the negotiation, the experienced diplomat knew when to play possum and maintain silence to gain leverage.
Suspecting danger, the rabbit played possum by freezing in place, hoping the predator would lose interest and move on.

senses.definition: someone or something that looks frightening, dangerous, or strong while in reality, they are not

examples.x: The dictator's aggressive rhetoric turned out to be a "paper tiger" as his military lacked the necessary resources for a sustained conflict.
The formidable reputation of the undefeated champion proved to be a "paper tiger" when a skilled challenger emerged and defeated them.
The new company in the market appeared to be a "paper tiger" with flashy marketing but failed to deliver quality products and services.
The politician's promises of reform proved to be nothing more than a "paper tiger" as their actions contradicted their words.
The aggressive dog barked loudly but was ultimately a "paper tiger" when faced with a calm and assertive owner.

senses.definition: to act as if one is not afraid or worried

examples.x: Despite the challenging circumstances, the team leader decided to "whistle in the dark" and expressed unwavering confidence to motivate the team.
John tried to "whistle in the dark" by making light-hearted jokes to mask his anxiety during the nerve-wracking job interview.
Susan, feeling uneasy in the unfamiliar neighborhood, tried to "whistle in the dark" and walked briskly while pretending to be self-assured.
The CEO, aware of the company's financial challenges, chose to "whistle in the dark" during the shareholders' meeting, emphasizing future growth prospects to instill confidence.

senses.definition: to begin crying just to get others to sympathize with one or do what one asks of them

examples.x: He always turns on the waterworks whenever he doesn't get his way, hoping to manipulate others.
Sarah pretended to turn on the waterworks to get out of trouble, but her sister saw through her act and didn't believe her tears were genuine.

word_list.word: champagne taste on a beer budget
word_list.pronunciation: ʃæmpˈeɪn tˈeɪst ˌɑːn ɐ bˈɪɹ bˈʌdʒɪt

senses.definition: used when someone likes to have things so expensive they cannot possibly afford

examples.x: She wanted to buy designer clothes and dine at fancy restaurants, but with her student budget, she had champagne taste on a beer budget.
John dreamt of owning a luxury sports car, but his salary only allowed for a used economy car—he had champagne taste on a beer budget.
Despite his modest income, he insisted on staying at high-end hotels during his vacation, showcasing his champagne taste on a beer budget.
The couple had champagne taste on a beer budget, constantly longing for lavish vacations and extravagant experiences they couldn't afford.

senses.definition: to purposefully choose to behave in a specific manner to show a specific idea or leave a specific impression

examples.x: Despite his fatigue, the politician struck an attitude of confidence and determination during the campaign rally.
The artist struck an attitude of contemplation as they stood in front of their latest masterpiece, deep in thought.
The teacher encouraged the students to strike an attitude of curiosity and open-mindedness when approaching new subjects.
In order to negotiate effectively, the diplomat knew she had to strike an attitude of empathy and understanding towards the opposing party.
The CEO entered the boardroom, striking an attitude of authority and command, instantly capturing the attention of everyone present.
